title: NOC 72603 – Engineer officers, water transport | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Engineer Officers (NOC 72603) are the technical heartbeat of Canada’s maritime industry. As the global shipping sector moves toward decarbonization and hybrid propulsion, these officers are responsible for the operation, maintenance, and repair of increasingly complex main engines and auxiliary machinery. Classified as TEER 2, the role has evolved significantly in 2026, requiring expertise in LNG-fueled engines, digital engine-room monitoring systems, and automated ballast water treatment. With nearly 28-36% of the workforce aged 50 or older, Canada is facing a steady demand for new marine engineers to support its commercial fleets, the Canadian Coast Guard, and offshore energy projects.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Category2 (Occupations usually requiring college, apprenticeship, or supervisory experience)Major Group72 – Technical trades and transportation officers and controllersSub-Major Group726 – Transportation officers and controllers

common_job_titles: Coast Guard Vessel First Watchkeeping Engineer
duties: Machinery Operation: Operating main engines, boilers, steering machinery, motors, pumps, generators, and condensers.
Watchkeeping: Standing engine room watch and monitoring the performance of all auxiliary equipment using digital monitoring systems.
Maintenance & Repair: Inspecting and conducting emergency or scheduled repairs to all mechanical and electrical systems aboard the vessel.
Crew Supervision: Coordinating and supervising the activities of the engine room crew and apprentices.
Reporting: Maintaining detailed logs and preparing reports on engine performance, fuel consumption, and equipment failures.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is required.

Training: Completion of a three-year cadet program in marine engineering from an approved institute OR approximately three years of experience as an engine room crew member plus six months of formal training.
Certification: A Marine Engineer Officer Certificate of Competency (Fourth, Third, Second, or First Class) issued by Transport Canada is mandatory.
Sea Service: Mandatory minimum of 6 months of sea service and completion of a Transport Canada-approved Training Record Book for Engineers (TRBE) for initial certification.
Medical Fitness: A valid marine medical certificate is required, ensuring physical capability for the demanding engine room environment.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook is Moderate to Good, with specific provincial highlights:

British Columbia: High Opportunity. BC remains a primary hub for marine engineering, with a median annual salary of approximately $88,638. Employment is concentrated in the Mainland/Southwest (43%) and Vancouver Island/Coast (41%) regions, supporting extensive ferry and tugboat operations.

Ontario: Strong Regional Demand. In March 2026, Ontario issued targeted OINP invitations for skilled trades and regional positions. Under the Employer Job Offer stream, candidates in Eastern and Northern Ontario—key areas for Great Lakes shipping—received invitations with scores starting at 54–57.

Newfoundland and Labrador: Steady Demand. Driven by the offshore oil sector and provincial ferry services, the outlook remains stable. The Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP) continues to be a vital pathway for engineers in this region.

National Trend: The industry is expected to face a moderate risk of labour shortage through 2033. High-tier "Chief Engineers" are currently earning median wages of $37.00/hr to $45.00/hr, with top earners reaching over $74.00/hr.
Why Work in Canada as a Marine Engineer?
Lead Green Maritime Tech: Work with 2026-grade innovations, including hydrogen-fuel cells and dual-fuel internal combustion engines.
Comprehensive Benefits: Over 99.7% of workers in this NOC receive at least one type of non-wage benefit, including pension plans and full medical/dental coverage.
Elite Compensation: As a TEER 2 professional, your earning potential is high. Senior Chief Engineers in 2026 can earn upwards of $150,000 annually in specialized offshore roles.
Priority for PR: In 2026, this NOC is a core feature of OINP targeted draws and remains a priority under the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP).
pathways: OINP Employer Job Offer: In February and March 2026, OINP issued invitations to skilled trades and regional candidates with scores as low as 50–54. This is a prime route for engineers with a job offer from Ontario-based shipping companies.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A high-success pathway for marine engineers in NS, NB, PEI, or NL. AIP allows for faster processing for "High-Skilled" workers with a provincial endorsement.

Express Entry (Federal Skilled Worker): As a TEER 2 occupation, NOC 72603 is eligible for general draws. While not currently in the specific "Transport" category (which targets pilots/mechanics), the high skill level provides a strong competitive edge.

2026 TR to PR Pathway: A confirmed initiative targeting up to 33,000 temporary residents. Marine engineers currently working in Canada are classified as "High-Priority Essential Infrastructure Workers."
